inputs.userGoal: Create a real-time document Q&A API leveraging Azure OpenAI and Azure Cosmos DB, expose endpoints using Azure API Management, and enforce rate limits per user.

architecturePrinciples: Secure by default with managed identity
Per-user rate limiting at the API gateway
Low-latency document retrieval with Cosmos DB

reasoning.whyChosen: Azure OpenAI provides managed GPT model access for document Q&A with enterprise-grade SLAs. S0 is the standard production SKU. East US has broad OpenAI model availability.
alternativesConsidered: Azure AI Services (AIServices kind) — broader multi-service account but unnecessary scope for this workload
Self-hosted open-source LLM on AKS — higher operational burden and no SLA advantage
reasoning.tradeoffs: S0 is pay-per-token with no reserved capacity discount; for high-volume workloads, consider provisioned throughput units (PTU) later.

references.title: Bicep resource reference — Cognitive Services accounts (2025-06-01)

resources.name: oai-docqa-prod-001/gpt-4o

reasoning.whyChosen: GPT-4o provides strong Q&A performance with lower latency and cost than GPT-4-turbo. 30K TPM capacity is a reasonable starting default for a production API.
alternativesConsidered: GPT-4-turbo — higher cost, marginally better on some reasoning tasks
GPT-3.5-turbo — cheaper but lower quality answers for complex documents
reasoning.tradeoffs: Standard deployment uses token-based billing; provisioned throughput (PTU) offers predictable latency at higher commitment.

resources.dependencyReasoning: Model deployment is a child resource of the OpenAI account.

reasoning.whyChosen: Cosmos DB NoSQL API with serverless capacity provides low-latency JSON document storage and native vector search for document embeddings. Serverless is ideal for variable/unpredictable workloads with pay-per-request pricing. Session consistency balances performance and correctness for Q&A workflows.
alternativesConsidered: Azure SQL Database — strong relational model but less natural for semi-structured documents and vector search
Azure AI Search — excellent for full-text and vector search but adds another service; Cosmos DB vector search consolidates storage and search
reasoning.tradeoffs: Serverless is single-region only and cannot use shared throughput databases. If traffic becomes predictable and high-volume, switch to provisioned throughput with autoscale.

reasoning.whyChosen: B1 Linux plan is a cost-effective starting tier that supports always-on and custom domains. Suitable for initial production with straightforward vertical scaling to Standard/Premium when needed.
alternativesConsidered: Azure Container Apps — serverless containers with auto-scaling, but adds complexity for a single API
Azure Functions Consumption — event-driven but cold starts impact real-time Q&A latency
reasoning.tradeoffs: B1 does not support deployment slots or autoscale. Upgrade to S1+ for staging slots or P1v3+ for autoscale and zone redundancy.

references.title: Bicep resource reference — App Service Plan (2024-11-01)

resources.name: app-docqa-prod-001

reasoning.whyChosen: App Service hosts the Q&A API backend with managed TLS, built-in auth hooks, and easy deployment via GitHub Actions or ZIP deploy. Python 3.12 is the standard runtime for OpenAI SDK workloads.
alternativesConsidered: Azure Container Apps — more flexibility for containerized workloads but heavier setup
Azure Functions — good for event-driven but less suited for stateful API patterns
reasoning.tradeoffs: App Service is PaaS with less container flexibility than Container Apps. For microservices or multi-container needs, consider migrating to Container Apps.

resources.dependencyReasoning: The web app runs on the App Service Plan and connects to OpenAI for inference and Cosmos DB for document storage.

resources.name: apim-docqa-prod-001

reasoning.whyChosen: API Management Consumption tier provides a serverless API gateway with built-in rate-limiting policies (rate-limit-by-key), subscription key management, and usage analytics — all with zero standing cost. Per-user rate limiting is enforced via APIM policies keyed on subscription ID or JWT claims.
alternativesConsidered: APIM Developer tier — includes a developer portal and more policy options but has a fixed monthly cost even at zero traffic
Azure Front Door with WAF — provides global load balancing and DDoS protection but lacks native API-level rate-limit-by-key policies
reasoning.tradeoffs: Consumption tier has cold-start latency on first call and lacks VNet integration, built-in cache, and developer portal. Upgrade to BasicV2/StandardV2 for those features.

resources.dependencyReasoning: APIM fronts the App Service backend; the API definition routes requests to the web app endpoint.

overallReasoning.summary: This architecture pairs Azure OpenAI (GPT-4o) with Cosmos DB serverless for a low-latency, pay-per-use document Q&A backend. App Service hosts the Python API, and API Management Consumption tier acts as the gateway enforcing per-user rate limits via rate-limit-by-key policies. All resources are co-located in East US for minimal latency. The serverless/consumption choices across Cosmos DB and APIM keep costs proportional to actual usage.
overallReasoning.tradeoffs: Serverless Cosmos DB is single-region; if multi-region HA is needed, switch to provisioned throughput. APIM Consumption tier has cold-start latency and no VNet support; upgrade to StandardV2 for enterprise networking. B1 App Service Plan lacks autoscale — upgrade to S1+ or P1v3 for production scaling.

plan.validation: All resources are in eastus. Cosmos DB serverless is single-region (matching the single location entry). APIM Consumption does not require VNet. App Service B1 supports always-on. OpenAI S0 with Standard deployment is compatible. No conflicting pairing constraints detected.

references.title: API Management rate limiting best practices
Cosmos DB vector search for AI applications
